verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: However, let him act in these matters as he himself thinks lawful or expedient.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: Let me recapitulate in the end of this book what I have said in a scattered way in my own defence.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: He had said of me that it seemed as if I could not be a heretic without him; I therefore set forth my belief and, in respect of the resurrection of the dead I proved that he rather than I was in error, since he spoke of the resurrection body as frail.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: I shewed also that he did away with the distinction of sex in the other world, saying that bodies would become souls women men.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: I next revealed the causes which had led to my translation—very proper causes in my opinion; I shewed that it was not because I was stimulated by contentiousness, nor because I was desirous of glory, but because I was incited by the fear of God, that I imported a store of old Greek material to be used in the new Latin construction, that I furbished up the old armour which had become enveloped in rust, not with a view to excite a civil war but to repel a hostile attack.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: I then introduced the chief matter on which they have laid their forgers’ hands, the adulterous blasphemy against the Son of God and the Holy Spirit, a thing quite alien from me, but brought in by these men in their wickedness as I shewed by quotations.
